message.content: Look at this repo, which is a recent fork from judell/bram where I'm just trying to switch it from GitHub to Entire. However, as I've been using Bram I've not found it to help me be mindful as the author states. Have one subagent read the code in this repo, and one read the README and the blog posts that it links to. Then synthesize the information and tell me: how is Bram supposed to make me more mindful when doing agentic engineering vs just using Claude Code without Bram? How am I e.g. supposed to be more aware of the code that it spits out as per the "“Doctor, it hurts when agents create unreviewable PRs.” “Don’t do that.”" blog post. Tell me what Jon Udell claims in his code and articles and tell me if what you find agrees with it or not.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codebase-analyzer: Analyzes codebase implementation details. Call the codebase-analyzer agent when you need to find detailed information about specific components. As always, the more detailed your request prompt, the better! :) (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

- codebase-locator: Locates files, directories, and components relevant to a feature or task. Call `codebase-locator` with human language prompt describing what you're looking for. Basically a "Super Grep/Glob/LS tool" — Use it if you find yourself desiring to use one of these tools more than once. (Tools: Grep, Glob, LS)

- codebase-pattern-finder: codebase-pattern-finder is a useful subagent_type for finding similar implementations, usage examples, or existing patterns that can be modeled after. It will give you concrete code examples based on what you're looking for! It's sorta like codebase-locator, but it will not only tell you the location of files, it will also give you code details! (Tools: Grep, Glob, Read, LS)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- posthog:error-analyzer: Analyze multiple PostHog errors in parallel to identify patterns, root causes, and prioritize fixes based on user impact. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)
- thoughts-analyzer: The research equivalent of codebase-analyzer. Use this subagent_type when wanting to deep dive on a research topic. Not commonly needed otherwise. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

- thoughts-locator: Discovers relevant documents in thoughts/ directory (We use this for all sorts of metadata storage!). This is really only relevant/needed when you're in a reseaching mood and need to figure out if we have random thoughts written down that are relevant to your current research task. Based on the name, I imagine you can guess this is the `thoughts` equivilent of `codebase-locator` (Tools: Grep, Glob, LS)

- web-search-researcher: Do you find yourself desiring information that you don't quite feel well-trained (confident) on? Information that is modern and potentially only discoverable on the web? Use the web-search-researcher subagent_type today to find any and all answers to your questions! It will research deeply to figure out and attempt to answer your questions! If you aren't immediately satisfied you can get your money back! (Not really - but you can re-run web-search-researcher with an altered prompt in the event you're not satisfied the first time) (Tools: WebSearch, WebFetch, TodoWrite, Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

## claude.ai Honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- slack:channel-digest: Get a digest of recent activity across multiple Slack channels
- slack:draft-announcement: Draft a well-formatted Slack announcement and save it as a draft
- slack:find-discussions: Find discussions about a specific topic across Slack channels
- slack:standup: Generate a standup update based on your recent Slack activity
- slack:summarize-channel: Summarize recent activity in a Slack channel
- posthog:llma-cc-ingest: Manually send a Claude Code session log to PostHog LLM Analytics
- posthog:llma-cc-setup: Set up PostHog LLM Analytics to capture Claude Code sessions
- posthog:llma-cc-status: Check if Claude Code sessions are being sent to PostHog LLM Analytics
- slack:block-kit: Help developers build and validate Block Kit layouts for Slack messages, modals, and Home tabs. Provides authoritative block references and validates with the blocks.validate API. Use this skill whenever the developer wants to compose Slack message layouts, build modals/forms/dialogs, design Home tab interfaces, create interactive messages with buttons or menus, modify existing Block Kit JSON, or asks about any Slack UI component (sections, actions, inputs, headers, alerts, tables, carousels). Also trigger when they mention "blocks", "Block Kit Builder", or paste JSON containing block structures like `"type": "section"`.
- slack:create-slack-app: Guide developers through creating a Slack app or agent using the Slack CLI and Bolt (JS or Python). Handles prerequisites, sandbox setup, authentication, project creation from templates, and local development.
- slack:slack-api: Discover, navigate, and call Slack Web API methods (the family.method endpoints at slack.com/api like chat.postMessage, conversations.history, users.info, views.open). Use this skill whenever the developer asks which Slack API method does something, needs a method's required OAuth scopes or token type, wants to call or test a Web API method, is handling cursor pagination (next_cursor), hitting rate limits (tier/ratelimited/Retry-After), or debugging API errors like missing_scope, invalid_auth, or channel_not_found. Also trigger when they paste a slack.com/api/ URL or a docs.slack.dev/reference/methods link, or ask how to list/fetch/post/update Slack resources via the API. This skill covers the Web API method layer: finding the right method, reading its contract (scopes, arguments, errors), and calling it over raw HTTP with curl or through a Slack SDK.
- slack:slack-cli: Use the Slack CLI to create, run, and manage Slack apps from the terminal. Use whenever the developer wants to log in, add a team, switch workspaces, or authenticate with Slack; whenever Slack CLI commands are needed (local development with `slack run`, managing app lifecycle, the manifest); or when searching the Slack developer documentation for any topic (socket mode, the Events API, OAuth, manifests, Bolt).
- slack:slack-messaging: Guidance for composing well-formatted, effective Slack messages using standard markdown
- slack:slack-search: Guidance for effectively searching Slack to find messages, files, channels, and people
- planetscale:mysql: Plan and review MySQL/InnoDB schema, indexing, query tuning, transactions, and operations. Use when creating or modifying MySQL tables, indexes, or queries; diagnosing slow/locking behavior; planning migrations; or troubleshooting replication and connection issues. Load when using a MySQL database.
- planetscale:neki: Overview and information about Neki, the sharded Postgres product by PlanetScale. Load when working with Neki-related tasks and the need to scale or shard postgres. Load when facing Postgres scaling or sharding issues.
- planetscale:postgres: PostgreSQL best practices, query optimization, connection troubleshooting, and performance improvement. Load when working with Postgres databases.
- planetscale:vitess: Vitess best practices, query optimization, and connection troubleshooting for PlanetScale Vitess databases. Load when working with Vitess databases, sharding, VSchema configuration, keyspace management, or MySQL scaling issues.
- posthog:analyzing-experiment-session-replays: Analyze session replay patterns across experiment variants to understand user behavior differences. Use when the user wants to see how users interact with different experiment variants, identify usability issues, compare behavior patterns between control and test groups, or get qualitative insights to complement quantitative experiment results.
- posthog:assessing-heatmaps: Assesses what a page's heatmap is telling you and recommends concrete changes. Pulls click / rageclick / scroll-depth data for a URL, names the hot elements by cross-referencing autocapture events on the same page, and can create a saved heatmap the user opens in PostHog, then summarizes the behavior and proposes improvements.
TRIGGER when: user asks what a heatmap shows, why people aren't clicking something, where users rage-click, how far they scroll, what to change on a page based on heatmap/click data, or to 'analyze/assess/review the heatmap' for a URL.
DO NOT TRIGGER when: the user only wants to create a saved heatmap screenshot with no analysis (use heatmaps-saved-create directly), or is asking about session replay in general (use investigating-replay).
- posthog:auditing-endpoints: Audit every endpoint in a PostHog project for staleness, failed materialisations, and unused materialised versions. Use when the user asks "what endpoints can I clean up?", "are any of my endpoints broken?", "which materialised versions are still being called?", or wants a one-shot cleanup pass over the Endpoints product. Produces a prioritised report grouped by issue type, with recommended actions but does not modify anything without explicit confirmation.
- posthog:auditing-experiments-flags: Audit PostHog experiments and feature flags for configuration issues, staleness, and best-practice violations. Read when the user asks to audit, health-check, or review experiments or feature flags, check flag hygiene, or verify experiment setup.
- posthog:auditing-warehouse-source-health: Audit the health of a PostHog project's data warehouse sources and syncs — find every broken or degraded source connection, sync schema, and webhook channel. Use when the user asks "why are my imports failing?", "what's broken with my sources?", "why is my warehouse data stale?", or wants a one-shot triage of source/sync health before deciding where to dig in. Produces a prioritized report grouped by severity, with recommended next steps. For materialized-view health use `auditing-warehouse-view-health`; for a single failing sync use `diagnosing-failed-warehouse-syncs`.
- posthog:auditing-warehouse-view-health: Audit the health of a PostHog project's materialized views (saved queries) — find every failed materialization and flag unused or stale materialized views that cost storage and compute. Use when the user asks "which of my views are broken?", "why is this materialized view failing?", "are any of my views wasting compute?", or wants a one-shot triage of view health. For source/sync health use `auditing-warehouse-source-health`.
- posthog:authoring-error-tracking-alerts: Author error tracking alerts that fire when an issue is created, reopened, or starts spiking. Use when the user asks to set up error notifications, route exceptions to Slack/webhook/Linear, or evaluate which error events are worth alerting on. Covers trigger-event selection, integration choice, dedup against existing alerts, and shipping with the canonical message body shape.
- posthog:authoring-log-alerts: Author useful, low-noise log alerts on services in a PostHog project. Use when the user asks to set up alerts for their logs, suggest alerts they should add, or evaluate whether a service is worth monitoring. Covers service triage, baseline characterisation, threshold drafting, back-testing via simulate, and shipping with a notification destination.
- posthog:authoring-scouts: How to author, edit, and adapt PostHog Signals scouts — the scheduled agents that scan a project and emit findings into the Signals inbox. Use when a user wants to customize a canonical scout for their own setup (narrow its scope, retune its thresholds, add disqualifiers), tweak a scout's schedule or dry-run posture, or write a brand-new scout from scratch for a specific use case (a custom event, a product surface no canonical scout covers). Covers the scout SKILL.md anatomy, the emit contract, the dedupe + scratchpad-memory conventions, the per-team skills-store path vs the canonical in-repo path, and the emit-and-inspect test loop (with dry-run as an optional safety net). Trigger on "write/edit/customize a signals scout", "new scout for X", "tune my scout schedule", "make a scout that watches <event>".
- posthog:building-workflows: Build, edit, test, enable, and monitor PostHog workflows over MCP. Author the action/edge graph so it runs and opens cleanly in the visual editor, then change drafts surgically with patch operations. Use when asked to build, set up, automate, change, fix, or debug a workflow, campaign, broadcast, drip sequence, or event-triggered automation in the workflows product.
- posthog:checking-deploy-timing: Determine when a PostHog code change reached a given environment by reading the hidden GIT deploy annotations in the project and correlating them with the merge commit on GitHub. Use when PostHog staff ask "when was X deployed", "is my change live in the US/EU yet", "has my PR shipped", "did the fix roll out to prod-us", or otherwise want to know whether/when a commit, PR, or feature went out to a region. Do not answer deploy-timing questions from event/data volume alone — that only shows when data changed, not when code shipped.
- posthog:choosing-trend-or-slope-view: Clarify how to visualize change over a time range before building a trend. Use whenever the user asks how much something changed, grew, dropped, improved, or regressed between two points or periods — "how much did X change from A to B", "before vs after", "start vs end", "week over week", "compare this month to last", "change over time" — or mentions a "slope chart" / "slopegraph". Two readings of "change" need different charts: the whole trend (a line, every interval) versus just the two endpoints (a slope, start vs end). Ask which they want, then render it. Not for choosing a saved insight ChartDisplayType in the insight editor.
- posthog:cleaning-up-stale-feature-flags: Identify and clean up stale feature flags in a PostHog project. Use when the user wants to find unused, fully rolled out, or abandoned feature flags, review them for safety, and then disable or delete them. Covers staleness detection, dependency checking, and safe removal workflows.
- posthog:configuring-experiment-analytics: Configures the analytics side of a PostHog experiment — exposure criteria (default `$feature_flag_called` vs custom exposure events), primary and secondary metrics, the supported metric types (count, sum, ratio with `math` and `math_property`, retention with `retention_window_start` and `start_handling`), multivariate user handling ("Exclude" vs "First seen variant"), and how to read results once the experiment is live. Use when the user adds or edits a primary or secondary metric (e.g. "add a secondary metric tracking 'downloaded_file' per user"), sets up a ratio metric (e.g. "revenue from purchase_completed / pageviews"), sets up a retention metric (e.g. "$pageview → uploaded_file, 7-day window"), configures custom exposure (e.g. "only count users who hit /checkout"), changes multivariate handling, or asks "who is in the analysis?", "how do I measure impact?", "is this winning?", "what's the confidence level?", or "should I ship?".
- posthog:configuring-experiment-rollout: Configures the rollout shape of a PostHog experiment — the variant split (50/50, 80/20, A/B/C ratios), the overall rollout percentage that gates how many users enter the experiment, and the disambiguation when a percentage like "roll out to 25%" could mean either. Use when the user mentions a rollout percentage, variant split, or traffic distribution; gives a ratio like 60/40, 70/30, or 80/20; asks "who sees the test variant?"; wants to increase, decrease, or change the rollout or split on a draft or running experiment; weighs equal vs uneven splits; or proposes a mid-experiment split change (often an anti-pattern that needs reset or end-and-restart).
- posthog:consuming-endpoints-from-client-code: Wire a PostHog endpoint into a client app or SDK. Covers fetching the OpenAPI spec, generating a typed client with openapi-generator or @hey-api/openapi-ts, sending the right auth header, shaping the variables payload (HogQL code_name vs insight breakdown property), handling rate-limit and materialised-endpoint error responses. Use when the user says "how do I call my endpoint", "generate a client for this", or "what auth header do I use".
- posthog:copying-flags-across-projects: Copy a feature flag from one PostHog project to one or more target projects in the same organization. Use when the user wants to duplicate a flag, promote a flag from staging to production, sync flags across projects, or replicate a flag configuration in a different workspace. Covers cohort remapping, scheduled-change handling, encrypted payloads, and the safe defaults (disabled in target, no scheduled changes).
- posthog:creating-ai-subscription: Create a recurring AI-generated PostHog report — schedule a free-text prompt to run on a cron, with the LLM-synthesized markdown delivered to email or Slack on each tick. Use when the user wants a recurring AI summary of X on any cadence (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly) rather than a one-off report. (To attach an AI summary to an existing insight/dashboard subscription instead of a free-text prompt, see `managing-subscriptions` and its `summary_enabled` option.)
- posthog:creating-an-endpoint: Create a PostHog endpoint with the right shape on the first try — covers query kind choice, name conventions, what to expose as variables (HogQL code_name vs insight breakdown), data_freshness_seconds, and whether to materialise on day one. Use when the user says "create an endpoint", "expose this query as an API", "turn this insight into an endpoint", or asks for help structuring a new endpoint. Steers away from common mistakes: materialising a query with cohort breakdowns or compare mode, inline-only variables on a materialised endpoint, unbounded date ranges, ambiguous names.
- posthog:creating-experiments: Guides agents through the 3-step experiment creation flow: defining the hypothesis, configuring rollout, and setting up analytics. Delegates rollout decisions to configuring-experiment-rollout and metric setup to configuring-experiment-analytics.
TRIGGER when: user asks to create a new experiment or A/B test, OR when you are about to call experiment-create.
DO NOT TRIGGER when: user is updating an existing experiment, managing lifecycle, or only browsing experiments.
- posthog:creating-replay-vision-scanners: Guides agents through creating and safely sizing a Replay Vision scanner: choosing the scanner type (monitor/classifier/scorer/summarizer), shaping the RecordingsQuery that selects sessions, and — crucially — estimating observation volume and checking the org's monthly quota before creating, so a broad scanner doesn't exhaust the budget on its first scheduled sweep.
TRIGGER when: user asks to create, set up, or configure a Replay Vision scanner, OR when you are about to call vision-scanners-create, OR when widening an existing scanner's query or sampling_rate via vision-scanners-update.
DO NOT TRIGGER when: only reading scanners or observations, deleting a scanner, or running an existing scanner against a single session on demand (vision-scanners-scan-session).
- posthog:debugging-local-replay: Debugs why session recordings aren't appearing in the local dev environment. Use when a developer reports that local replay ingestion isn't working, recordings aren't showing up despite /s calls, or the replay pipeline seems broken after hogli start. Covers the full local pipeline: SDK capture, Caddy proxy, capture-replay (Rust), Kafka, ingestion-sessionreplay (Node), recording-api (Node), SeaweedFS, and common failure modes like orphaned processes, stuck phrocs workers, and trigger misconfiguration.
- posthog:debugging-signals-pipeline: Debug the signals pipeline locally end-to-end. Covers emitting test signals from fixtures, monitoring Temporal workflows via the REST API, reading sandbox agent logs from object storage, inspecting Docker sandbox containers, and diagnosing common failures (stale ClickHouse embeddings, agentsh network denials, inactivity timeouts). Use when a signal isn't reaching the inbox, a signal-report-summary workflow fails, or a sandbox task run times out.
- posthog:debugging-surveys: Debug, support, and build PostHog Surveys across the backend and all five SDKs (web/posthog-js, iOS, Android, Flutter, React Native). Use whenever a Surveys support ticket is pasted ("survey not showing", "fewer responses than expected", "responses disappeared", "survey shows on wrong platform"), when diagnosing why a survey does or doesn't display, or when doing survey feature work that must ship across SDKs. Covers the eligibility pipeline, cross-SDK feature parity, the known-cause catalog, read-only diagnostic queries, staff access, and the customer-reply style guide.
- posthog:designing-email-templates: Author, save, and edit email templates in the PostHog workflows library — compose email design JSON with Liquid personalization and create and round-trip-edit templates over MCP. Use when asked to design, build, update, or fix an email template for workflows, broadcasts, or campaigns.
- posthog:diagnosing-ci-and-merge-bottlenecks: Diagnoses CI and pull-request pipeline health for a GitHub repo using the engineering analytics MCP tools — pull-requests (PR list with CI status), workflow-health (per-workflow CI trends), and pr-lifecycle (a single PR's timeline). Use when asked whether CI is getting faster or slower, which GitHub Actions workflow is the slow or flaky long-pole, how long PRs take from open to merge, how an author's merge time compares to the cohort, which open PRs have failing or pending CI, or where a specific pull request is stuck. Triggers on "engineering analytics", "is CI getting slower", "slow workflow", "flaky CI", "time to merge", "cycle time", "PR throughput", "failing checks", "where is PR <n> stuck", "CI long pole", "what's holding up this PR".

# Jon Udell's claims: how Bram makes agentic coding more mindful

Sources: the Bram README (`/Users/peter/code/bram/README.md`) plus the four blog posts it links, all on `blog.jonudell.net`. The key post the user named is the June 28 "unreviewable PRs" post.

## The problem: unreviewable PRs

Udell's starting pain is agent output no human can meaningfully review, and the industry's reflex of automating the review away.

- "When agents raise PRs with thousands of lines of LLM-written adds/deletes/edits, people can't make sense of them." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- The critiqued industry fix: "The solution? Throw more agents at the problem: reviewer agents that scan what coding agents have produced, identify problems, and triage them." (unreviewable-PRs post) — the title's joke ("Doctor, it hurts…" / "Don't do that") is the rebuttal: don't generate unreviewable PRs in the first place.

Framed more broadly in the June 2 git/GitHub post: agent-assisted development is "chaotic," and Bram exists to make it "safe, orderly, and accountable."

## Core claim: stay fully engaged, read the code as it's written

The central mindfulness claim is that the workflow keeps the human continuously engaged rather than rubber-stamping a giant diff at the end.

- "as I use Bram to bootstrap itself, I am fully engaged thanks to the workflow embodied in the tool." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "I have yet to write a single line of Rust! But I read the Rust code that Claude Code and Codex write for me, as they write it. I understand the nature and purpose of that code, and I push back when things don't smell right." (unreviewable-PRs post)

The README compresses this into the tagline "Bram runs agents mindfully" (README line 7).

## The worklist and small reviewable increments

The worklist is the mechanism that keeps diffs small enough to actually review, and forces deliberation at each transition.

- "Bram's workflow helps do that by breaking problems into small testable chunks and processing them in an orderly way." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "It helps you think clearly about the work you are doing, and proceed in well-defined chunks and sequences." (git/GitHub post)
- The three-phase gated flow: "Things you are working on show up on the Worklist and pass through three phases: proposed → applied → committed. The arrows between the phases are approval gates where you can dwell and iterate with your agents on what you are planning to build, or what you have built and are testing." (git/GitHub post)

The README mirrors this exactly (lines 75-85): "proposed → applied → committed… approval gates where you can dwell and iterate," and notes that by default *every* change request flows through the worklist (with explicit opt-outs).

## How Bram makes you more aware of the agent's code

Two levers: a self-created scope contract, and a legible display that beats the raw terminal.

Scope contract (from "Vibe coding as a team sport," June 17):
- At the To-Apply gate you "Approve, Iterate, or Drop." Before that gate: "Up to this point Bram has made no changes to tracked files in the repository. Now it begins to do so, constrained by a self-created list of the files it expects to touch." — i.e., the agent commits up front to which files it will change, so surprises are visible.
- "When the agent proposes a change, the diff is easier to read than in the terminal," and while agents work "you can open and review the item's plan."

Legibility (from "What is the terminal?," July 1, and the team-sport post): Bram grew out of watching non-coders who "did not enjoy reading the terminal to try making sense of what Claude Code was doing." Bram gives "a more legible display of: agent messages, your messages, pasted screenshots, diffs, tool calls and results," "echoes agent responses more readably than they appear in the terminal," and "reports recent tool uses as a compact list of links." It also makes git/GitHub commands "fluent without having to remember command syntax," so attention goes to decisions rather than syntax.

## Accountability, durable context, and the auditable trail

Udell's mindfulness argument is partly social: pull the private LLM chat context out into shared, reviewable spaces.

- "Chat sessions build context that's private to LLMs, not shared with a team of people and agents. Bram lifts that context into two kinds of shared spaces: the local worklist and the GitHub repository." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "It makes context durable in GitHub, so prior work (and discussion about work) is available to people and agents as new work intersects with old." (git/GitHub post)
- "Decisions and tactics aren't squirreled away in [a] dot file on a per-machine-per-user basis. They are accessible to the whole team both interactively and by means of gh APIs that were formerly daunting but now easily wielded by agents on our behalf." (git/GitHub post)

The README echoes the accountability thesis (line 13): GitHub is "great for accountability… orderly and well-documented collaboration that leaves an auditable trail."

## Reframing "human in the loop"

Udell explicitly rejects the passive framing that mindfulness is usually pitched against.

- "I dislike the phrase 'human in the loop' because it cedes authority to the machines. Let's flip the narrative. It's our loop, we work the same way we always have, now we recruit agents to join the team." (unreviewable-PRs post)

The team-sport post extends "our loop" to multiple people and agents: agents introduce themselves and comment on shared GitHub issues, so "your team now includes not only you and your agents but also your human team members and their agents" — vibe coding as "a team sport."

## Notes / caveats

- The "What is the terminal?" (July 1) post is mostly about legibility and the terminal being "now optional"; it does not add worklist/mindfulness claims beyond the display argument.
- The README also links one non-Udell "Elsewhere" post (waltzweb.wordpress.com, "GitHub for today's hybrid teams") — I did not fetch it since the task targets Udell's claims; flag if you want it read too.
- All quotes above are as returned by fetches of the live posts; where exactness matters for publication, worth a final eyeball against the source URLs (the June 2 post has a minor typo in the original: "dot file").

- https://blog.jonudell.net/2026/06/17/vibe-coding-as-a-team-sport/
- https://blog.jonudell.net/2026/07/01/what-is-the-terminal/</result>

# Jon Udell's claims: how Bram makes agentic coding more mindful

Sources: the Bram README (`/Users/peter/code/bram/README.md`) plus the four blog posts it links, all on `blog.jonudell.net`. The key post the user named is the June 28 "unreviewable PRs" post.

## The problem: unreviewable PRs

Udell's starting pain is agent output no human can meaningfully review, and the industry's reflex of automating the review away.

- "When agents raise PRs with thousands of lines of LLM-written adds/deletes/edits, people can't make sense of them." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- The critiqued industry fix: "The solution? Throw more agents at the problem: reviewer agents that scan what coding agents have produced, identify problems, and triage them." (unreviewable-PRs post) — the title's joke ("Doctor, it hurts…" / "Don't do that") is the rebuttal: don't generate unreviewable PRs in the first place.

Framed more broadly in the June 2 git/GitHub post: agent-assisted development is "chaotic," and Bram exists to make it "safe, orderly, and accountable."

## Core claim: stay fully engaged, read the code as it's written

The central mindfulness claim is that the workflow keeps the human continuously engaged rather than rubber-stamping a giant diff at the end.

- "as I use Bram to bootstrap itself, I am fully engaged thanks to the workflow embodied in the tool." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "I have yet to write a single line of Rust! But I read the Rust code that Claude Code and Codex write for me, as they write it. I understand the nature and purpose of that code, and I push back when things don't smell right." (unreviewable-PRs post)

The README compresses this into the tagline "Bram runs agents mindfully" (README line 7).

## The worklist and small reviewable increments

The worklist is the mechanism that keeps diffs small enough to actually review, and forces deliberation at each transition.

- "Bram's workflow helps do that by breaking problems into small testable chunks and processing them in an orderly way." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "It helps you think clearly about the work you are doing, and proceed in well-defined chunks and sequences." (git/GitHub post)
- The three-phase gated flow: "Things you are working on show up on the Worklist and pass through three phases: proposed → applied → committed. The arrows between the phases are approval gates where you can dwell and iterate with your agents on what you are planning to build, or what you have built and are testing." (git/GitHub post)

The README mirrors this exactly (lines 75-85): "proposed → applied → committed… approval gates where you can dwell and iterate," and notes that by default *every* change request flows through the worklist (with explicit opt-outs).

## How Bram makes you more aware of the agent's code

Two levers: a self-created scope contract, and a legible display that beats the raw terminal.

Scope contract (from "Vibe coding as a team sport," June 17):
- At the To-Apply gate you "Approve, Iterate, or Drop." Before that gate: "Up to this point Bram has made no changes to tracked files in the repository. Now it begins to do so, constrained by a self-created list of the files it expects to touch." — i.e., the agent commits up front to which files it will change, so surprises are visible.
- "When the agent proposes a change, the diff is easier to read than in the terminal," and while agents work "you can open and review the item's plan."

Legibility (from "What is the terminal?," July 1, and the team-sport post): Bram grew out of watching non-coders who "did not enjoy reading the terminal to try making sense of what Claude Code was doing." Bram gives "a more legible display of: agent messages, your messages, pasted screenshots, diffs, tool calls and results," "echoes agent responses more readably than they appear in the terminal," and "reports recent tool uses as a compact list of links." It also makes git/GitHub commands "fluent without having to remember command syntax," so attention goes to decisions rather than syntax.

## Accountability, durable context, and the auditable trail

Udell's mindfulness argument is partly social: pull the private LLM chat context out into shared, reviewable spaces.

- "Chat sessions build context that's private to LLMs, not shared with a team of people and agents. Bram lifts that context into two kinds of shared spaces: the local worklist and the GitHub repository." (unreviewable-PRs post)
- "It makes context durable in GitHub, so prior work (and discussion about work) is available to people and agents as new work intersects with old." (git/GitHub post)
- "Decisions and tactics aren't squirreled away in [a] dot file on a per-machine-per-user basis. They are accessible to the whole team both interactively and by means of gh APIs that were formerly daunting but now easily wielded by agents on our behalf." (git/GitHub post)

The README echoes the accountability thesis (line 13): GitHub is "great for accountability… orderly and well-documented collaboration that leaves an auditable trail."

## Reframing "human in the loop"

Udell explicitly rejects the passive framing that mindfulness is usually pitched against.

- "I dislike the phrase 'human in the loop' because it cedes authority to the machines. Let's flip the narrative. It's our loop, we work the same way we always have, now we recruit agents to join the team." (unreviewable-PRs post)

The team-sport post extends "our loop" to multiple people and agents: agents introduce themselves and comment on shared GitHub issues, so "your team now includes not only you and your agents but also your human team members and their agents" — vibe coding as "a team sport."

## Notes / caveats

- The "What is the terminal?" (July 1) post is mostly about legibility and the terminal being "now optional"; it does not add worklist/mindfulness claims beyond the display argument.
- The README also links one non-Udell "Elsewhere" post (waltzweb.wordpress.com, "GitHub for today's hybrid teams") — I did not fetch it since the task targets Udell's claims; flag if you want it read too.
- All quotes above are as returned by fetches of the live posts; where exactness matters for publication, worth a final eyeball against the source URLs (the June 2 post has a minor typo in the original: "dot file").

- https://blog.jonudell.net/2026/06/17/vibe-coding-as-a-team-sport/
- https://blog.jonudell.net/2026/07/01/what-is-the-terminal/</result>

## How Bram structurally forces human review into the agent loop

Bram wraps Claude Code / Codex in a Tauri shell whose entire design converts "agent silently edits files" into "agent must propose, wait, and be gated." The mechanisms split cleanly into **ENFORCED** (a hook or Rust host physically blocks/reverts the action) and **CONVENTION** (CLAUDE.md / conventions.md instruct, but nothing blocks).

---

### 1. The worklist lifecycle — the core gate

Lifecycle: **propose → user triages → approve → apply → (separate) commit → prune**. Prose lives in `resources/worklist-drafts/<id>.md`; metadata in `resources/worklist.json`. The two-stage split (`proposed` = "TO APPLY", `applied` = "TO COMMIT") gives the user two distinct veto points: once before code is touched, once before it lands as a commit (`app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui:579`).

**ENFORCED:**

- **No file edit without coverage.** `app/__shell/worklist-guard.py:588-600` (Claude PreToolUse) denies any `Write`/`Edit` to a project file unless (a) the path is listed in a `proposed`/`applied` worklist item (`worklist_covered_files`, lib line 297-316), (b) a fresh 1h `direct-edit` bypass record exists (`fresh_bypass`, :319-333), or (c) the last user message contains a literal opt-out phrase (`_OPT_OUT_PATTERNS`, :179-187 — "just do it", "skip the worklist", etc.). Otherwise `sys.exit(2)` blocks the tool call (`deny_coverage`, :336-361). The Codex equivalent `app/shell/worklist-guard-codex.py` covers `apply_patch`, `Bash` (to stop `sed -i`/`tee` end-runs), and `mcp__filesystem__*` writes.
- **Prose can't hide in the metadata index.** Both guards reject a `worklist.json` write that puts non-empty inline `before`/`after` on a proposed item (`worklist_items_with_inline_prose` / `deny_inline_prose`, :267-294, :426-447). Forces review prose into the draft file.
- **Agents can't self-advance state.** Direct edits to `worklist.json` that remove an item or change its `status` are denied (`deny_mechanical_worklist_change`, :450-482). State transitions must go through `POST /__worklist/mutate`.
- **Optimistic-concurrency version guard.** Every `worklist.json` write must bump the top-level `version` by exactly 1 or it's denied as `stale-worklist-version` (:380-409, :570-577) — prevents concurrent-writer clobber between agent and host.
- **Watcher reverts unauthorized prunes.** Even if a write slips past the hook, the filesystem watcher calls `maybe_enforce_worklist_policy` (lib `src-tauri/src/lib.rs:23300`); an item removal not backed by a matching auth record causes the host to rewrite the prior file contents back to disk (`:23356-23368`, "reverting unauthorized removal ... via watcher fallback"). Defense-in-depth behind the hook.

**Authorization is structural, not a text signal:**
- The `approved:`/`drop:` line the user's button emits is NOT authority by itself. The host records clicked ids into `resources/.worklist-authorization.json` with a `kind`. `/__worklist/resolve` is the only channel that hands the agent the recorded item bodies, and records are **consumed on first read** (`handle_worklist_resolve`, lib:25922; `consume_worklist_authorization` at :26002).
- `/__worklist/mutate` re-validates: `advance` requires an `approved` auth record covering every id; `prune` requires `drop` (or `approved` for post-commit prune) — `validate_worklist_mutate_authorization` (:26243-26268), and post-commit prune additionally requires the items be `applied` status (`validate_post_commit_prune_status`, :26279-26298).

**CONVENTION:** "Default: every change request goes through the worklist ... size doesn't matter" (conventions.md). The propose-first discipline itself is instruction; the hook only enforces coverage *once the agent tries to write*. Investigation work is told to stay out of the worklist and Drop if nothing to commit.

---

### 2. The commit gate

**ENFORCED** by `handle_worklist_commit` (lib:26725):
- Requires an `approved` auth covering every id (`ensure_worklist_commit_authorized`, :26313-26331).
- **Stages only the approved items' files** and refuses to commit if *any unrelated file is already staged* — `ensure_no_unrelated_staged_files` (:26776, :26788) returns "refusing worklist commit because unrelated files are staged." This prevents an agent from sweeping stray changes into an approved commit.
- Path traversal / absolute paths rejected (`validate_worklist_commit_paths`, :26333).
- Commits with the exact approved file list, then prunes the items in the same call.

So a commit is scoped to exactly what the user approved — the agent cannot broaden it.

---

### 3. Commit / git etiquette — mostly CONVENTION

These shape behavior via CLAUDE.md/conventions.md instructions (no hook enforces them):
- **"Don't nudge toward commit approval"** — a TO COMMIT item sits indefinitely; agent describes state factually and stops.
- **"Don't infer commit/drop/advance from feedback"** — "looks good"/"it works" are explicitly NOT authorization. (This is *reinforced* structurally: the opt-out regex `:179-187` deliberately omits passive-approval phrases, so even the hook won't treat "go ahead" as a bypass.)
- **Hold the commit while a related TO APPLY is in flight**; **warn when a new item entangles a TO COMMIT** (files-overlap check); **suggest a branch** before risky/multi-commit work; **notice squashable sibling commits**.
- **Close-on-commit** IS partly enforced: `closesIssues` drives a confirm dialog (`Workspace.xmlui:853+`), and issue closing goes through the verified host route `handle_issue_close_json` / `gh_issue_close_with_commit` (lib:7014-7096), which pushes-then-verifies GitHub sees the commit before closing and refuses (`commit-not-visible`) rather than blindly closing. The Push button auto-rebases on non-fast-forward.
- **ENFORCED anti-footgun:** the guard blocks the `gh ... --body @file` antipattern on any Bash call (`is_gh_body_at_antipattern`, worklist-guard.py:96-105, :492-513).

---

### 4. Visibility tabs — what the user SEES (the "mindfulness" surface)

The agent pane (`app/tools/Main.xmlui:333-341`) is a fixed set of read-mostly windows onto the agent, each backed by a host DataSource route:

- **Worklist** (`Workspace.xmlui`) — pending items with TO APPLY / TO COMMIT badges, per-item Approve / Iterate / Drop buttons, and an **inline diff** of the actual on-disk change (`DiffView`, :765-772) plus before/after prose. This is the primary review surface: the user reads the diff before approving.
- **Transcript** (`Transcript.xmlui`) — renders the live session's events (user turns, tool calls with summaries) from `/__sessions/list` + JSONL, so the user watches what the agent did.
- **Commits** (`Commits.xmlui`) — `/__commits`, `/__git/status`; shows unpushed count, Push (auto-rebasing) and Pull buttons, commit search. (Note: conventions tell the agent NOT to quote unpushed counts in chat — the tab is the source of truth.)
- **Sessions / History** — browse/rename/delete past sessions and turns.
- **Context** (`Context.xmlui`) — searchable view of CLAUDE.md + memory + hooks + settings, so the user can see what instructions are shaping the agent.
- **Status** (`Status.xmlui`) — `/__coordination-status` dashboard: worklist state, inflight sentinel, hooks, authorization, turn-completion detector, session size. This is where the user diagnoses whether the gate machinery itself is healthy.
- **Issues** — GitHub issues (polls every 30s).

**Skip worklist** button (`Main.xmlui:461`) is the sanctioned escape hatch — it prepends the `skip-worklist:` prefix and the host writes a one-turn direct-edit bypass, so the user *deliberately* opts out per-turn rather than the agent deciding to.

---

### 5. The inflight sentinel — a visible "agent is mid-cycle" lock

`resources/.inflight-claim.json`, written/cleared only by host handlers (`write_inflight_claim_sentinel` lib:18467, cleared by `/__worklist/mutate`), drives the Worklist spinner. A stuck spinner is described in conventions as "the convention enforcing itself" — it signals the user that an approve/drop/iterate cycle hasn't mechanically completed, so the loop can't silently proceed. Host completion detectors (Claude JSONL `stop_reason:end_turn`, Codex `task_complete`, PTY silence) clear it, but only after checking JSONL says the turn is actually final (`[agent-status] op=skip-sentinel-clear ... reason=jsonl-non-final`).

---

### 6. Permission-menu surfacing — keeps native prompts visible

`app/__shell/permission-menu-hook.py` is OBSERVE-ONLY: on Claude's `PermissionRequest`/`AskUserQuestion` it POSTs the pending permission dialog to `/__menu/permission` so the agent pane can render the permission choice as data (instead of the user having to read the raw xterm grid). It never decides — the human still answers. This means Claude Code's own tool-permission prompts remain a human checkpoint, surfaced cleanly.

---

### Summary: ENFORCED vs CONVENTION

**Physically blocked/reverted (hooks + Rust host):**
- File edits without worklist coverage / bypass / opt-out phrase (both agents, incl. Bash & MCP end-runs on Codex).
- Inline prose in the metadata index.
- Agent self-advancing worklist state (mutate is the only channel; auth consumed on read; watcher reverts unauthorized prunes).
- Version-bump concurrency guard.
- Commits scoped to approved files only; refuses unrelated staged files.
- Issue-close verified against GitHub before closing.
- `gh --body @` antipattern.

**Asked-for behavior only (instructions):**
- Propose-first as the default posture (the hook only bites at write time).
- Don't nudge toward / don't infer commit approval (reinforced by the opt-out regex deliberately excluding passive phrases).
- Branch suggestions, squash detection, entanglement warnings, matching prose verbosity, referring to items by id.

The net effect vs raw Claude Code: an agent that in a bare terminal would edit-and-move-on is here forced to write a reviewable proposal, surface a real diff in the Worklist tab, stop, and wait for an explicit button-click that the host records and consumes — with a filesystem watcher reverting anything that slips the hook. The human is inserted at propose-time, apply-time, and commit-time, and can watch the whole thing through the Transcript/Commits/Status tabs.

Key files: `app/__shell/worklist-guard.py`, `app/shell/worklist-guard-codex.py`, `app/__shell/permission-menu-hook.py`, `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` (routes ~25922-26844, revert ~23300-23369), `app/tools/Main.xmlui`, `app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui`, and the design philosophy in `/Users/peter/code/bram/CLAUDE.md` + `app/__shell/conventions.md`.</result>

content.text:
## What Udell claims (blog + README)

**The pain:** agents raise PRs with thousands of LLM-written lines nobody can review. Industry "fix" = throw reviewer-agents at it. His rebuttal (the title joke): don't generate unreviewable PRs in the first place.

**His mindfulness thesis, in his words:**
- *"as I use Bram to bootstrap itself, I am fully engaged thanks to the workflow embodied in the tool."*
- *"I have yet to write a single line of Rust! But I read the Rust code that Claude Code and Codex write for me, as they write it... and I push back when things don't smell right."*
- The fix for big diffs: *"breaking problems into small testable chunks and processing them in an orderly way."*
- The worklist gates: *"proposed → applied → committed. The arrows between the phases are approval gates where you can dwell and iterate."*
- Reframes human-in-the-loop: *"It's our loop... now we recruit agents to join the team."*
- Accountability: chat context is private to the LLM; Bram *"lifts that context into two kinds of shared spaces: the local worklist and the GitHub repository."*

Sources: blog.jonudell.net posts of 2026-06-28 (unreviewable PRs), 06-02 (git/GitHub), 06-17 (team sport), 07-01 (terminal).

## What the code actually does

The claims aren't just vibes. The code **physically enforces** most of them. Split:

**ENFORCED (hook blocks or host reverts):**
- **No file edit without a worklist item covering that path.** `worklist-guard.py:588-600` → `sys.exit(2)`. This is the mechanism that turns "agent edits and moves on" into "agent must propose first." Escape hatches are explicit: `skip-worklist:` prefix, opt-out phrases ("just do it"), or a fresh bypass record.
- **Two veto points, structurally.** `proposed`=TO APPLY (before code touched), `applied`=TO COMMIT (before it lands). Agent **can't self-advance** — only `/__worklist/mutate` moves state, and it re-checks a recorded button-click auth that's consumed on read (`lib.rs:26243`).
- **Commit scoped to exactly approved files.** Refuses if unrelated files are staged (`ensure_no_unrelated_staged_files`, `:26776`). Agent can't smuggle extra changes into your approved commit.
- **Watcher reverts** unauthorized prunes even if a write slips the hook (`lib.rs:23356`).
- Prose forced into review draft, not hidden in metadata index. Issue-close verified against GitHub before closing.

**CONVENTION only (instructions, no block):**
- Propose-first as default posture (hook only bites at write-time).
- **"Don't nudge toward commit approval" / "don't infer commit from 'looks good'"** — but *reinforced* structurally: the opt-out regex deliberately omits passive-approval phrases, so even the hook won't treat "go ahead" as bypass.
- Branch suggestions, squash detection, entanglement warnings.

**The awareness surface (how you "read code as it's written"):** Worklist tab shows an **inline diff of the real on-disk change** + before/after prose per item, with Approve/Iterate/Drop. Transcript tab renders agent turns/tool-calls more legibly than raw xterm. This is the concrete answer to "how am I more aware": the diff is put in front of you at the approval gate, one small chunk at a time, and you can't advance past it without a click the host records.

## Verdict: does code agree with claims?

**Yes, strongly.** Udell's claims map almost 1:1 onto enforced mechanisms:

| Claim | Backed by |
|---|---|
| "small testable chunks, orderly" | worklist coverage hook + version guard |
| "approval gates where you dwell" | proposed/applied two-stage, auth consumed on read |
| "read the code as it's written" | Worklist inline DiffView per item |
| "context lifted into shared spaces" | worklist.json + GitHub routes, not private chat |
| "I push back when things don't smell right" | Iterate button, no auto-advance |

The one honest gap: the parts about *not rushing you* ("don't nudge toward commit," "don't infer approval") are **convention, not enforcement** — an agent that ignored CLAUDE.md could still pester you to approve. But even there the design leans in: the bypass regex won't accept passive phrases, so sloppy "ok fine" can't be laundered into authorization.

## Why you may not feel more mindful

Two likely reasons, given your fork:
1. **You're editing Bram itself / doing setup work**, where you probably lean on `skip-worklist:` and opt-out phrases — which turns the whole gate off. The mindfulness only kicks in when changes actually route through the worklist.
2. The GitHub→Entire switch means the **accountability half** (durable context in GitHub, issue-close, PR trail) is exactly what you're ripping out/replacing — so you lose the "shared space" leg of Udell's thesis and keep only the local worklist leg.

Want me to check how much of your recent work actually went through the worklist vs. bypassed it? That'd show whether you're getting the mindfulness the design intends or routing around it.

Checked. Verdict: you're actually using it correctly, and the machinery IS engaging.

**Your 3 commits:**

| Commit | Went through worklist? | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `0416d74` Phase 1 (Sessions from checkpoints) | ✅ yes | multiline body, *"Part of the entire-sessions-checkpoints-migration worklist item"*, Co-Authored-By, Entire-Checkpoint trailer |
| `8676dcf` Phase 2 (label sessions) | ✅ yes | same shape, *"Phase 2 of the sessions/checkpoints migration"* |
| `2804319` Add entire | ❌ bypass | bare one-word message, no body, no trail — a `skip-worklist:`/opt-out direct edit |

**Read:** 2 of 3 are textbook worklist-disciplined — small, phased (Phase 1 → Phase 2), each referencing its driving item, reviewable diffs. The one bypass (`Add entire`) is trivial (drop in the CLI), exactly the kind of thing the opt-out exists for. So you're not routing around the gates — you split the migration into reviewable chunks and let each pass the gates. That's precisely Udell's *"small testable chunks... proceed in well-defined chunks and sequences."*

**Why you may not feel mindful despite doing it right:**
1. **Sample is tiny.** 3 commits total (rest of the 1008 are Udell's upstream). Not enough reps for the discipline to feel like it's earning its keep yet.
2. **No runtime forensics here.** Worklist is empty, no auth file, `resources/bram-traces/` doesn't exist in this checkout. Items prune after commit, so the "gate" moment is gone the instant you approve — the diff you dwelled on isn't preserved anywhere you'd re-encounter it. The mindfulness is *in the moment*, then invisible.
3. **You're the reviewer of your own migration.** Udell's thesis has two legs — small chunks (local worklist) *and* shared accountability (GitHub trail, teammates' agents commenting). Solo on a fork, you only get leg one. The "team sport" payoff isn't there, so it feels like ceremony rather than collaboration.

**Bottom line:** the code does what Udell claims, and your commits prove you're using it as intended. The flat feeling is dosage + solo context, not that Bram is failing to make you mindful. If you want the discipline to *feel* like it's paying off, the missing piece is the accountability half — which is exactly what your GitHub→Entire switch is rebuilding. Want me to check whether the Entire migration preserves that shared-context leg (checkpoints/trails as the GitHub-issue replacement)?

content.text:
Worklist is **gitignored** (`.gitignore:44` — *"Worklist proposals are per-developer scratch; keep them out of the repo"*), so `git pull` will never bring it. It lives only on your other laptop. This machine has none.

## Transfer = copy 2 paths off the other laptop

Copy these from the other laptop into `/Users/peter/code/bram/`:

```
resources/worklist.json          # the item metadata + version
resources/worklist-drafts/       # the before/after prose per item
```

Optionally also (only if you want the audit trail / in-flight feedback):
```
resources/worklist-history/      # past decisions
resources/feedback-drafts/       # unresolved iterate feedback
```

Any transport works — rsync/scp over the network, or AirDrop the two paths. E.g. from the other laptop:

```
rsync -av ~/code/bram/resources/worklist.json \
          ~/code/bram/resources/worklist-drafts/ \
   peter@THIS-LAPTOP:~/code/bram/resources/
```

## Do NOT copy these (machine-local runtime state — copying breaks things)

```
resources/.bram-port  .bram-port.json  .inflight-claim.json
resources/.worklist-authorization.json  .worklist-intent.json  .worklist-result.json
```

Those are host/PTY state for the *running* Bram on the other machine. Bram here regenerates them.

## Two reconciliation gotchas after copy

1. **`version` field** in `worklist.json` — fine to carry over as-is; the host treats whatever's on disk as the baseline and bumps from there. No action needed.
2. **The `migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` umbrella is now partly done.** Commit `d4a523e` (Issues read path) already landed on main since that item was written. So the copied item's draft prose will describe work that's partly complete. Once it's here, iterate the draft to strike the finished read-path scope — otherwise you'll re-propose work that's already committed.

Want me to do the reconciliation for you once you've copied the files — diff the umbrella draft against what `d4a523e` and the two Phase commits already landed, and rewrite the `after` prose to just the remaining scope (write path: `/__issue/comment`, `/__issue/close`, close-on-commit)?

Now the install. Key point: the README's `curl ... judell/bram/releases` installs the **upstream** prebuilt binary — the opposite of what you want. Your fork's changes live in `app/` + `src-tauri/`, and Bram embeds `app/` into the binary at build time, so **the only way to run your version is to build from this repo's source.** `cargo` isn't installed here yet. Let me check what's already present.

```
./bram
```

from `/Users/peter/code/bram`. That's guaranteed to be your fork — it's built from this repo's `src-tauri/` and embeds this repo's `app/` tree at build time. Nothing upstream is on your PATH (`which bram` = not found), so there's no shadowing.

Two rebuild tiers (from the project's own rules):

| You edit | To see it |
|---|---|

file.content:
# Before

The Issues tab now reads from Entire trails (committed read path), but
every user-facing label still says "Issues" / "issue". Confirmed live:
the tab renders a real trail ("#2 Test trail", *evjan opened 1 minute
ago*) under the heading **Issues**, with a **+ New issue** button, an
**Issues** nav item, and a search placeholder reading "Search title,
body, comments, author…". So the data is trails but the vocabulary is
GitHub issues.

Label locations:

- `app/tools/Main.xmlui:335` — `<NavLink label="Issues" to="/issues" />`
  (left-nav item).
- `app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` — heading "Issues", "+ New issue"
  button, "opened" / "comments" wording, search placeholder, empty
  state, and the expanded-detail copy.

# After

Relabel the **user-facing** strings from "Issues"/"issue" to
"Trails"/"trail":

- `Main.xmlui`: NavLink `label="Issues"` → `label="Trails"`.
- `Issues.xmlui`: heading → "Trails"; "+ New issue" → "+ New trail";
  search placeholder → drop "comments" (trails have findings, not
  comments) e.g. "Search title, body, author…"; "opened" wording and
  empty-state text → trail phrasing; expanded-detail copy.

Deliberately **out of scope** (kept as-is to avoid churn and risk with
no user-visible payoff):

- Internal identifiers — component name `Issues`, file `Issues.xmlui`,
  route `/issues`, `var.newIssueText` etc., and the backend `/__issues*`
  route names. These aren't visible; renaming them is pure churn and
  would risk the working read path. Can be revisited later if desired.
- The "comments" concept itself. The read path returns `comments: []`;
  wiring trail **findings** into that surface is part of the deferred
  write-path item, not this cosmetic pass.

XMLUI-only, so it hot-reloads — no rebuild. No file overlap with the
`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` item (that's `lib.rs`), so the two
are independent.

147	            variant="{newIssueVoiceRecording ? 'solid' : 'outlined'}"
148	            enabled="{!isWorklistVoiceProcessingTarget('new-issue')}"
149	            onClick="setWorklistVoiceTarget('new-issue'); newIssueVoiceRecording = toggleVoiceForCurrentTarget(newIssueVoiceRecording)" />
150	          <Theme size-Spinner="14px">
151	            <Spinner when="{isWorklistVoiceProcessingTarget('new-issue')}" />
152	          </Theme>
153	        </HStack>
154	        <HStack gap="$space-2">
155	          <Button
156	            label="Send"
157	            enabled="{!newIssueSubmitting && newIssueText && newIssueText.trim().length > 0}"
158	            onClick="newIssueStartSig = lastIssuesSig; newIssueTurnEnded = false; newIssueSubmitting = true; toTurn('File a GitHub issue: ' + (newIssueText || '').trim()); showNewIssueHint = false; newIssueText = ''; newIssueVoiceRecording = false; setWorklistVoiceTarget('message-agent')" />
159	          <Button
160	            label="Cancel"
161	            variant="ghost"
162	            onClick="showNewIssueHint = false; newIssueText = ''; newIssueVoiceRecording = false; setWorklistVoiceTarget('message-agent')" />
163	        </HStack>
164	      </VStack>
165	    </VStack>
166	  </ModalDialog>
167	
168	  <SearchHitModal
169	    id="searchHitModalRef"
170	    query="{query}" />
171	
172	  <PushSource
173	    id="issuesAgentTurnEndEvt"
174	    initial="{null}"
175	    subscribe="{window.bramSubscribeTauriEvent('agent-turn-end')}" />
176	  <ChangeListener
177	    listenTo="{issuesAgentTurnEndEvt.value && issuesAgentTurnEndEvt.value.tick}"
178	    onDidChange="() => { if (newIssueSubmitting) { newIssueTurnEnded = true; issuesFirstPage.refetch(); issuesFull.refetch(); } }" />
179	  <HSplitter
180	    height="100%"
181	    initialPrimarySize="{window.__bramRestoreSplitterSize('issues', '40%')}"
182	    onResize="(px) => window.__bramSaveSplitterSize('issues', px)">
183	
184	    <VStack padding="$space-4" gap="$space-4" overflowY="auto">
185	      <HStack gap="$space-3" verticalAlignment="center">
186	        <Heading level="h2">Issues</Heading>
187	        <Button
188	          when="{repoOrigin.value && repoOrigin.value.issuesNewUrl}"
189	          label="+ New issue"
190	          size="xs"
191	          enabled="{!newIssueSubmitting}"
192	          onClick="showNewIssueHint = true" />
193	        <Theme size-Spinner="16px">
194	          <Spinner when="{newIssueSubmitting || (issuesFirstPage.inProgress && !issuesFirstPage.loaded)}" />
195	        </Theme>
196	        <SpaceFiller />
197	        <SearchBox
198	          placeholder="Search title, body, comments, authors…"
199	          inProgress="{searchResults.inProgress}"
200	          onQueryChange="(q) => query = q"
201	          onClear="expandedNumber = null; selectedHitLine = null; searchHitModalRef.close()" />
202	      </HStack>
203	
204	      <Text
205	        when="{!repoOrigin.value || !repoOrigin.value.htmlBase}"
206	        color="$textColor-secondary">
207	        No GitHub remote detected for this project. Add an origin remote (or run `gh repo set-default`) to populate this tab.
208	      </Text>
209	
210	      <Text
211	        when="{(!query || query.trim().length < 2) && repoOrigin.value && repoOrigin.value.htmlBase && issuesFirstPage.loaded && !issuesFirstPage.inProgress && (!issuesSource || issuesSource.length === 0)}"
212	        color="$textColor-secondary">
213	        No issues found. (Either the repo has none, or `gh` is not installed / authenticated.)
214	      </Text>
215	
216	      <Text
217	        when="{query && query.trim().length >= 2 && searchResults.value && (!searchResults.value.results || searchResults.value.results.length === 0) && !searchResults.inProgress}"
218	        color="$textColor-secondary">
219	        No matches.
220	      </Text>
221	
222	      <VStack gap="0" width="100%" when="{displayedIssues.length > 0}">
223	        <HStack
224	          when="{(!query || query.trim().length < 2)}"
225	          gap="$space-3"
226	          paddingHorizontal="$space-3"
227	          paddingVertical="$space-2"
228	          backgroundColor="$color-surface-100"
229	          borderBottom="1px solid $borderColor">
230	          <Link
231	            when="{!expandedNumber}"
232	            width="64px"
233	            noIndicator
234	            onClick="const r = window.toggleSort(sortField, sortDir, 'state', 'desc'); sortField = r.field; sortDir = r.dir">
235	            <Text fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold" fontSize="$fontSize-sm" color="$textColor-secondary"
236	              value="{window.sortLabel('STATE', sortField, sortDir, 'state')}" />
237	          </Link>
238	          <Link
239	            width="*"
240	            noIndicator
241	            onClick="const r = window.toggleSort(sortField, sortDir, 'number', 'asc'); sortField = r.field; sortDir = r.dir">
242	            <Text fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold" fontSize="$fontSize-sm" color="$textColor-secondary"
243	              value="{window.sortLabel('TITLE', sortField, sortDir, 'number')}" />
244	          </Link>
245	          <Link
246	            when="{!expandedNumber}"
247	            width="90px"
248	            noIndicator
249	            onClick="const r = window.toggleSort(sortField, sortDir, 'updatedAt', 'desc'); sortField = r.field; sortDir = r.dir">
250	            <Text fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold" fontSize="$fontSize-sm" color="$textColor-secondary"
251	              value="{window.sortLabel('AGE', sortField, sortDir, 'updatedAt')}" />
252	          </Link>
253	        </HStack>
254	        <Items data="{displayedIssues}">
255	          <VStack gap="0" borderBottom="1px solid $borderColor" var.issueNumber="{$item.number}">
256	            <HStack
257	              gap="$space-3"
258	              paddingHorizontal="$space-3"
259	              paddingVertical="$space-2"
260	              verticalAlignment="center">
261	              <HStack when="{!expandedNumber}" width="64px" verticalAlignment="center">
262	                <Text
263	                  fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
264	                  fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold"
265	                  color="{$item.state === 'OPEN' ? '$color-success-700' : '$textColor-secondary'}"
266	                  value="{$item.state === 'OPEN' ? 'open' : 'closed'}" />
267	              </HStack>
268	              <HStack width="*" gap="$space-2" verticalAlignment="center">
269	                <Link
270	                  onClick="expandedNumber = $item.number; selectedHitLine = null"
271	                  noIndicator>
272	                  <VStack gap="0">
273	                    <Text
274	                      value="#{$item.number} {$item.title}"
275	                      fontWeight="{expandedNumber === $item.number ? '$fontWeight-bold' : '$fontWeight-normal'}"
276	                      maxLines="1" />
277	                    <Text
278	                      when="{$item.commentSummary}"
279	                      fontSize="$fontSize-xs"
280	                      color="$textColor-secondary"
281	                      maxLines="1"
282	                      value="{'comments: ' + $item.commentSummary}" />
283	                    <Text
284	                      fontSize="$fontSize-xs"
285	                      color="$textColor-secondary"
286	                      maxLines="1"
287	                      value="{(($item.author && ($item.author.login || $item.author.name)) || 'unknown') + ' opened ' + getDateUntilNow($item.createdAt) + ' ago'}" />
288	                    <Text
289	                      when="{$item.latestCommentAuthor && $item.latestCommentAt}"
290	                      fontSize="$fontSize-xs"
291	                      color="$textColor-secondary"
292	                      maxLines="1"
293	                      value="{$item.latestCommentAuthor + ' commented ' + getDateUntilNow($item.latestCommentAt) + ' ago'}" />
294	                  </VStack>
295	                </Link>
296	              </HStack>
297	              <HStack when="{!expandedNumber}" width="90px" verticalAlignment="center">
298	                <Text fontSize="$fontSize-sm" color="$textColor-secondary" value="{getDateUntilNow($item.activityAt || $item.updatedAt || $item.createdAt)}" />
299	              </HStack>
300	            </HStack>
301	
302	            <VStack
303	              when="{$item.hits && $item.hits.length > 0}"
304	              gap="0"
305	              paddingHorizontal="$space-3"
306	              paddingBottom="$space-2">
307	              <Items data="{$item.hits}" var.parentIssue="{$item}">
308	                <Link
309	                  onClick="searchHitModalRef.open(searchHitBestBody(query, [parentIssue.body, $item.snippet, parentIssue.title]), '#' + parentIssue.number + ' ' + parentIssue.title)">
310	                  <HStack gap="$space-2">
311	                    <Text
312	                      value="{$item.field}"
313	                      fontSize="$fontSize-xs"
314	                      color="$textColor-secondary"
315	                      width="56px" />
316	                    <Text
317	                      value="{$item.snippet}"
318	                      fontSize="$fontSize-xs"
319	                      whiteSpace="nowrap"
320	                      overflowMode="ellipsis"
321	                      maxLines="1" />
322	                  </HStack>
323	                </Link>
324	              </Items>
325	            </VStack>
326	          </VStack>
327	        </Items>
328	        <HStack
329	          when="{(!query || query.trim().length < 2) && issuesSource && issuesSource.length > 20}"
330	          padding="$space-2"
331	          horizontalAlignment="center">
332	          <Link
333	            onClick="issuesExpanded = !issuesExpanded"
334	            noIndicator>
335	            <Text
336	              fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
337	              value="{issuesExpanded ? 'Show fewer' : 'More (' + (issuesSource.length - 20) + ' more)'}" />
338	          </Link>
339	        </HStack>
340	        <Text
341	          when="{query && query.trim().length >= 2 && searchResults.value && searchResults.value.truncated}"
342	          paddingHorizontal="$space-3"
343	          paddingVertical="$space-2"
344	          color="$textColor-secondary"
345	          fontSize="$fontSize-xs">
346	          (results truncated)
347	        </Text>
348	      </VStack>
349	    </VStack>
350	
351	    <VStack when="{expandedNumber}" padding="$space-4" gap="$space-3" overflowY="auto">
352	      <HStack gap="$space-2" verticalAlignment="center">
353	        <Text
354	          when="{selectedIssue}"
355	          fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
356	          fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold"
357	          color="{selectedIssue.state === 'OPEN' ? '$color-success-700' : '$textColor-secondary'}"
358	          value="{selectedIssue.state === 'OPEN' ? 'open' : 'closed'}" />
359	        <Link
360	          when="{selectedIssue}"
361	          width="*"
362	          noIndicator
363	          onClick="selectedIssue.url ? openExternal(selectedIssue.url) : null">
364	          <Text
365	            fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold"
366	            value="#{selectedIssue.number} {selectedIssue.title}"
367	            maxLines="2" />
368	        </Link>
369	        <Button
370	          label="✕"
371	          variant="ghost"
372	          size="xs"
373	          tooltip="Close detail pane"
374	          onClick="expandedNumber = null; selectedHitLine = null" />
375	      </HStack>
376	      <VStack when="{selectedIssue}" gap="0">
377	        <Text
378	          fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
379	          color="$textColor-secondary"
380	          value="{((selectedIssue.author && (selectedIssue.author.login || selectedIssue.author.name)) || 'unknown') + ' opened ' + getDateUntilNow(selectedIssue.createdAt) + ' ago'}" />
381	        <Text
382	          when="{selectedIssue.latestCommentAuthor && selectedIssue.latestCommentAt}"
383	          fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
384	          color="$textColor-secondary"
385	          value="{selectedIssue.latestCommentAuthor + ' commented ' + getDateUntilNow(selectedIssue.latestCommentAt) + ' ago'}" />
386	        <Text
387	          when="{selectedIssue.state === 'CLOSED' && selectedIssue.closedBy && selectedIssue.closedByAt}"
388	          fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
389	          color="$textColor-secondary"
390	          value="{'closed by ' + selectedIssue.closedBy + ' ' + getDateUntilNow(selectedIssue.closedByAt) + ' ago'}" />
391	      </VStack>
392	      <Link
393	        when="{selectedHitLine && query && query.trim().length >= 2}"
394	        onClick="selectedHitLine = null"
395	        noIndicator>
396	        <Text value="↥ Show full issue" fontSize="$fontSize-xs" />
397	      </Link>
398	      <Text when="{issueDetail.inProgress && !issueDetail.loaded}">Loading…</Text>
399	      <Markdown
400	        when="{issueDetail.value && issueDetail.value.body}"
401	        content="{issueDetail.value.body}" />
402	      <VStack
403	        gap="$space-2"
404	        when="{issueDetail.value && issueDetail.value.body && window.__bramExtractMarkdownImages(issueDetail.value.body).length > 0}">
405	        <Items data="{window.__bramExtractMarkdownImages(issueDetail.value.body)}">
406	          <Link onClick="fullscreenImage = $item" noIndicator>
407	            <Image
408	              src="{$item}"
409	              maxHeight="200px"
410	              fit="contain" />
411	          </Link>
412	        </Items>
413	      </VStack>
414	      <VStack
415	        gap="$space-3"
416	        when="{issueDetail.value && issueDetail.value.comments && issueDetail.value.comments.length > 0}">
417	        <Items data="{issueDetail.value.comments}">
418	          <VStack
419	            gap="$space-1"
420	            paddingTop="$space-2"
421	            borderTop="1px solid $borderColor">
422	            <Text
423	              fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
424	              color="$textColor-secondary"
425	              value="{(($item.author && ($item.author.login || $item.author.name)) || 'unknown') + ' commented ' + getDateUntilNow($item.createdAt) + ' ago'}" />
426	            <Markdown
427	              when="{$item.body}"
428	              content="{$item.body}" />
429	            <VStack
430	              gap="$space-2"
431	              when="{$item.body && window.__bramExtractMarkdownImages($item.body).length > 0}">
432	              <Items data="{window.__bramExtractMarkdownImages($item.body)}">
433	                <Link onClick="fullscreenImage = $item" noIndicator>
434	                  <Image
435	                    src="{$item}"
436	                    maxHeight="200px"
437	                    fit="contain" />
438	                </Link>
439	              </Items>
440	            </VStack>
441	          </VStack>
442	        </Items>
443	      </VStack>
444	      <VStack
445	        gap="$space-1"
446	        when="{issueDetail.value && issueDetail.value.crossReferences && issueDetail.value.crossReferences.length > 0}"
447	        paddingTop="$space-3"
448	        borderTop="1px solid $borderColor">
449	        <Text
450	          fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
451	          color="$textColor-secondary"
452	          value="Mentioned in {issueDetail.value.crossReferences.length} {issueDetail.value.crossReferences.length === 1 ? 'issue' : 'issues'}" />
453	        <Items data="{issueDetail.value.crossReferences}">
454	          <Link
455	            onClick="expandedNumber = $item.number; selectedHitLine = null; query = ''"
456	            noIndicator>
457	            <HStack gap="$space-2" verticalAlignment="center">
458	              <Text value="#{$item.number}" fontSize="$fontSize-sm" color="$textColor-secondary" />
459	              <Text
460	                fontSize="$fontSize-sm"
461	                fontWeight="$fontWeight-bold"
462	                color="{$item.state === 'OPEN' ? '$color-success-700' : '$textColor-secondary'}"
463	                value="{$item.state === 'OPEN' ? 'open' : 'closed'}" />
464	              <Text value="{$item.title}" />
465	            </HStack>
466	          </Link>
467	        </Items>
468	      </VStack>
469	      <VStack
470	        when="{selectedIssue}"
471	        gap="$space-2"
472	        paddingTop="$space-3"
473	        borderTop="1px solid $borderColor">
474	        <TextArea
475	          id="closeCommentBox"
476	          placeholder="Optional comment"
477	          autoFocus="true"
478	          autoComplete="off"
479	          autoCorrect="{false}"
480	          spellCheck="{false}"
481	          autoCapitalize="off"
482	          resize="vertical"
483	          minRows="2"
484	          maxRows="12"
485	          width="*"
486	          enabled="{!commentIssueCall.inProgress && !closeIssueCall.inProgress}" />
487	        <HStack gap="$space-2">
488	          <Button
489	            label="Comment"
490	            variant="outlined"
491	            size="sm"
492	            enabled="{!commentIssueCall.inProgress && !closeIssueCall.inProgress && closeCommentBox.value && closeCommentBox.value.trim().length > 0}"
493	            onClick="commentIssueCall.execute({ number: selectedIssue.number, body: closeCommentBox.value })" />
494	          <Button
495	            when="{selectedIssue.state === 'OPEN'}"
496	            label="Close issue"
497	            variant="outlined"
498	            size="sm"
499	            enabled="{!commentIssueCall.inProgress && !closeIssueCall.inProgress}"
500	            onClick="closeIssueCall.execute({ number: selectedIssue.number, comment: closeCommentBox.value || '' })" />
501	          <Theme size-Spinner="16px">
502	            <Spinner when="{commentIssueCall.inProgress || closeIssueCall.inProgress}" />
503	          </Theme>
504	        </HStack>
505	      </VStack>
506	    </VStack>
507	
508	  </HSplitter>
509	</Component>
